title: Diabetic Retinopathy -- Treatment / Management -- Management of Diabetic Macular Edema (DME)
content: Center-involving diabetic macular edema - Nowadays, anti-VEGF agents have become the first line of treatment for center-involving diabetic macular edema. Bevacizumab, ranibizumab, and aflibercept have beneficial effects in patients with baseline better visual acuity. Aflibercept is shown to have better visual outcomes in patients with worse baseline visual acuity on presentation. [51] Recently, aflibercept and ranibizumab have received FDA approval for use in both DME and diabetic retinopathy. [52] Intravitreal steroid implants also are used in recalcitrant cases. [53] Non-center involving macular edema - Focal or Grid laser-guided by ETDRS is the treatment of choice.

title: Diabetic Retinopathy -- Treatment / Management -- Management of Diabetic Macular Edema (DME)
content: Nowadays, in the era of Anti-VEGF medications, laser treatment is usually avoided in the center involving macular edema. It may be added as adjuvant therapy in patients not responding to anti-VEGF therapy alone. As stated by ETDRS, laser helped in halting the progression of moderate visual loss, but it did not improve the visual acuity. Bevacizumab intravitreal injection is also effective in the center-involving DME, but it is not FDA approved. Hence it is available only for off-label use. Cluster endophthalmitis due to fake drugs or other reasons is another concern. [54] DRCR (diabetic retinopathy clinical research) network studied the role of aflibercept in DME, and it was found to be useful in patients with initial worse visual acuity (Protocol T of DRCR.net). [51] Other notable studies other than the DRCR network on intravitreal Anti-VEGF injections included RISE, RIDE, READ, RESTORE, BOLT, and RESOLVE. Doses of commonly used intravitreal injections:

title: Macular Edema -- Treatment / Management -- Ocular Laser
content: Diabetic macular edema: Laser therapy is recommended for patients with inadequate response to anti-VEGF therapy. Healthcare providers typically utilize double diode or YAG photocoagulation lasers to treat diabetic macular edema. The ETDRS reveals that laser reduces the risk of moderate visual loss, or a loss of 15 letters on the EDTRS visual chart or a doubling of the visual angle, at 36 months. After 6 weeks, clinicians can treat any untreated initial lesions. Repeat treatment should be scheduled at least 4 months after the initial session if no initial lesions remain. [99] Diabetic macular edema treatment techniques: The various techniques to manage diabetic macular edema are listed below.

title: Diabetic Retinopathy -- Treatment / Management -- Management of Diabetic Macular Edema (DME)
content: Refractory Diabetic macular edema - The definition of refractory or persistent DME varies, including macular edema not responding to multiple injections of anti-VEGF and, at least one sitting of laser, and suboptimal response (central macular thickness on OCT of more than 250 microns with associated visual loss) to 6 consecutive monthly injections of anti-VEGF agents. [72] [73] A possible approach to such cases includes evaluating systemic control, higher dosing or frequency of anti-VEGF medications, use of steroid implants (according to protocol U of DRCR.net), rechecking of the diagnosis, ruling out any unfavorable anatomical causes, and ultrawide field angiography to look for extreme peripheral ischemic areas and to laser as far in the periphery as possible. [74] [71] [75]

title: Diabetic Macular Edema -- History and Physical
content: Patients with diabetes receive more frequent ophthalmic evaluations than the average person; usually, patients with diabetes should get eye exams every year. In certain instances, this may be more or less frequent). Diabetic macular edema is diagnosed stereoscopically, and the macula appears thicker with diabetic macular edema when compared to eyes not impacted by DME. The Early Treatment Retinopathy Study (ERDRS) has defined DME as a thickening of the retina or the presence of hard exudates within 1 disc diameter from the center of the macula, and this is the most commonly used definition. There are various patterns of thickening, including focal, multifocal, and diffuse areas. DME is deemed medically significant if it is associated with any of the following: thickening within 500 mm of the center of the macula,  the presence of hard exudates within 500 mm of the center of the macular if associated with thickening of the adjacent retinal tissue, or zones of retinal thickening 1 disk area of which part of which is within 1 disk diameter of the macular center. It is important to note that only of these features must be present in order for diabetic macular edema to be deemed as clinically significant, although it is not uncommon for several of these features to be present. [7] [9]

title: Macular Edema -- Evaluation -- Fundus Fluorescein Angiography
content: FFA can delineate regions of retinal capillary leakage. The Early Treatment Diabetic Retinopathy Study (ETDRS) categorizes diabetic macular edema into diffuse and focal types based on the degree of fluorescein leakage associated with microaneurysms. According to the ETDRS criteria, focal diabetic macular edema exhibits 67% or more leakage associated with microaneurysms, intermediate demonstrates 33% to 66% leakage, and diffuse showcases less than 33% leakage associated with microaneurysms. [68] Larssen et al define diffuse diabetic macular edema as retinal thickening affecting 2 or more disk areas with involvement of the macular center. Focal diabetic macular edema, on the other hand, presents retinal thickening of less than 2 disk areas without affecting the macular center. [69] In FFA, focal macular edema is identified by fluorescein leakage from specific capillary regions. Ischemic diabetic macular edema appears as hypofluorescent areas within the macula (see Image. Multimodal Imaging of Diabetic Macular Edema).

title: Diabetic Retinopathy -- Treatment / Management -- Management of Diabetic Macular Edema (DME)
content: Long-acting corticosteroid implants like dexamethasone implants and fluocinolone acetonide implants (Retisert and Iluvien) are also used in diabetic macular edema. Though they are not considered the first line of treatment. They are reserved for cases not responding to multiple injections of Anti-VEGF medications (according to protocol U of DRCR.net). [71] Their main advantage is long-term control of inflammation and a lesser frequency of administration. The high cost is also one of the factors for its limited use. Intravitreal injections of triamcinolone are not FDA approved, have poorer results, and are linked with more complications than anti-VEGF medications. Glaucoma is a significant side effect of intravitreal steroids. A new port-delivery system for sustained release of ranibizumab, has been approved to manage wet age-related macular degeneration by the FDA in 2021. Its promises to be a new therapy for DME.

title: Snellen Chart -- Definition/Introduction
content: Invented in 1862 by a Dutch ophthalmologist named Herman Snellen, the Snellen chart remains the most widespread technique in clinical practice for measuring visual acuity. [1] [2] The Snellen chart serves as a portable tool to quickly assess monocular and binocular visual acuity. Throughout history, there have been several charts utilized by eye care professionals. The most common in clinical practice is the Snellen chart, while research studies more often use logMAR charts, such as the Early Treatment of Diabetic Retinopathy Study (ETDRS) chart.

title: Optical Coherence Tomography -- Clinical Significance -- Macular Edema
content: OCT has revolutionized the diagnosis of macular edema. Macular edema can arise from various conditions, with diabetes mellitus being the most prevalent cause. [41] [42] Several OCT-based grading systems for diabetic macular edema have been proposed. OCT is also useful in evaluating uveitic macula edema due to any etiology, including Irvine-Gass syndrome. [43] Please see StatPearls' companion resources, " Diabetic Macular Edema ," " Macular Edema ," and " Uveitic Macular Edema ," for more information.

title: Macular Edema -- Treatment / Management -- Intravitreal Steroids
content: A phase III trial reveals that implantable devices containing either 0.2 or 0.5 μg/d produce superior visual improvement compared to sham injections for up to 3 years. Patients with chronic diabetic macular edema have superior outcomes. Almost all patients with phakic macular edema develop cataracts, but visual improvement is equivalent to pseudophakic macular edema following cataract surgery. The associated incidence of glaucoma is low at 7.6% in high-dose or 0.5 μg/d and 3.7% in the low-dose or 0.2 μg/d groups. [106]

title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 4 -- Community 2904
content: Diabetic retinopathy (اعتِلال الشبكية السُّكَّرِيّ) is clinically associated with end‑stage renal disease (مرض كلوي في المرحلة النهائية). Both conditions represent severe microvascular complications of long‑standing diabetes mellitus, and the presence of diabetic retinopathy often signals an increased risk of progression to advanced kidney failure. Recognizing this association is essential for clinicians, as early detection and management of retinal changes can prompt more aggressive monitoring of renal function, and coordinated care can help mitigate the morbidity associated with simultaneous vision loss and the need for renal replacement therapy.

title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 1231
content: The retina serves as the primary anatomic site for a variety of ocular pathologies, including retinal detachments, structural defects, vascular occlusions, and retinopathy. In patients with secondary diabetes mellitus, both non‑proliferative retinopathy and moderate non‑proliferative retinopathy are identified as retinal findings, underscoring that diabetic retinopathy manifests within the retinal tissue. Additionally, the condition referred to as داء ريفسام also presents its findings in the retina. Recognizing the retina as the common location for these disorders is essential for accurate diagnosis, monitoring, and management in ophthalmic and systemic disease care.

title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1171
content: Secondary diabetes mellitus is a subtype of diabetes mellitus without complication, positioned under the broader category “Secondary diabetes mellitus, without mention of complication.” This hierarchical relationship indicates that secondary diabetes mellitus inherits the characteristics of diabetes mellitus without complication while representing a more specific clinical entity. In some patients, secondary diabetes mellitus can evolve into a severe form identified as “secondary diabetes mellitus with diabetic peripheral angiopathy with gangrene,” reflecting the potential for peripheral vascular disease and tissue loss. Ocular complications that are directly caused by secondary diabetes mellitus include several grades of retinal disease: Mild nonproliferative retinopathy due to secondary diabetes mellitus, Moderate nonproliferative retinopathy due to secondary diabetes mellitus, Non‑proliferative retinopathy due to secondary diabetes mellitus, and Retinopathy due to secondary diabetes

title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 186
content: Secondary diabetes mellitus is a distinct form of diabetes mellitus that frequently leads to multiple organ complications, most notably retinal disease and nephropathy. It gives rise to a spectrum of retinal complications—including mild non‑proliferative retinopathy, moderate non‑proliferative retinopathy, non‑proliferative retinopathy, and broader retinopathy—all localized to the retina (Arabic: “داء ريفسام”). Additional retinal findings associated with this condition include retinal detachments, defects, vascular occlusions, and choroidal lesions. Therapeutic management of these ocular lesions often involves destructive procedures directed at the posterior segment of the eye, such as radiation‑guided destruction of localized retinal or choroidal lesions. Within secondary diabetes mellitus, two major sub‑classifications are recognized. One subtype lacks explicit complications and is classified as diabetes mellitus without complication. The other subtype is characterized by diabetic

title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2261
content: The retina serves as the anatomical site for a range of ocular pathologies, including retinal detachments, structural defects, vascular occlusion, and various forms of retinopathy. Both mild nonproliferative retinopathy due to secondary diabetes mellitus and non‑proliferative retinopathy due to secondary diabetes mellitus are identified on the retina, indicating that diabetic retinal changes are localized to this tissue. Additionally, the condition referred to in Arabic as داء ريفسام also has the retina as its finding site, further emphasizing the retina’s central role in ocular disease manifestation. Recognizing that these diverse abnormalities share the retina as their common location aids clinicians in targeted examination and management of retinal disorders.

title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 271
content: Secondary diabetes mellitus is a form of diabetes mellitus that is specifically associated with complications, and it encompasses a severe subtype characterized by diabetic peripheral angiopathy with gangrene. A broader category, secondary diabetes mellitus without mention of complication, is linked to diabetes mellitus without complication, indicating that when complications are not documented the condition aligns with uncomplicated diabetes. The presence of secondary diabetes mellitus gives rise to several microvascular complications: mild non‑proliferative retinopathy, moderate non‑proliferative retinopathy, non‑proliferative retinopathy, and general retinopathy, all of which are directly due to secondary diabetes mellitus. In addition, secondary diabetes mellitus can lead to nephropathy. title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 194
content: Secondary diabetes mellitus is a recognized subtype of diabetes mellitus that can appear with or without documented complications. When classified as secondary diabetes mellitus with complications, it may progress to diabetic peripheral angiopathy with gangrene, reflecting peripheral vascular disease and tissue loss, and it commonly generates microvascular complications such as mild, moderate, and non‑proliferative retinopathy and diabetic nephropathy. The retina is the principal site of ocular involvement, displaying findings that include retinal detachments, defects, vascular occlusion and other forms of retinopathy; these retinal changes are directly attributable to the metabolic disturbance of secondary diabetes mellitus. Uncontrolled disease‑condition determination, interpreted through the general clinical state, is specifically associated with diabetes mellitus with complications, underscoring the clinical importance of recognizing uncontrolled metabolic disease. title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 2 -- Community 967
content: Secondary diabetes mellitus is a form of diabetes that arises secondary to another condition. In the hierarchical classification, Secondary diabetes mellitus has the parent category Secondary diabetes mellitus, without mention of complication, which itself is a subcategory of diabetes mellitus without complication. Although it is generally described without specific complications, Secondary diabetes mellitus is also identified as a subtype that includes diabetic peripheral angiopathy with gangrene, reflecting the potential for severe vascular involvement. Clinical manifestations directly attributable to Secondary diabetes mellitus include Moderate nonproliferative retinopathy due to secondary diabetes mellitus, Non-proliferative retinopathy due to secondary diabetes mellitus, and Nephropathy due to secondary diabetes mellitus. These ocular and renal complications underscore the importance of monitoring and managing Secondary diabetes mellitus to prevent organ damage. Recognizing the hierarchical relationships among these diagnostic categories aids clinicians in accurate documentation and targeted patient care.

title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2625
content: Secondary diabetes mellitus, defined here as secondary diabetes mellitus with diabetic peripheral angiopathy and gangrene, is a form of diabetes that arises secondary to another underlying condition and is complicated by severe peripheral vascular disease and tissue necrosis. This underlying secondary diabetes mellitus directly gives rise to several microvascular complications: mild nonproliferative retinopathy due to secondary diabetes mellitus, non‑proliferative retinopathy due to secondary diabetes mellitus, and nephropathy due to secondary diabetes mellitus. The coexistence of diabetic peripheral angiopathy and gangrene further impairs blood flow, heightening the risk and severity of these ocular and renal manifestations. Recognizing that the retinopathies and nephropathy are attributable to the secondary diabetes mellitus, especially when accompanied by peripheral angiopathy and gangrene, guides clinicians to prioritize tight glycemic control, aggressive management of peripheral vascular disease, and early screening for eye and kidney involvement to improve patient outcomes.

title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2477
content: Secondary diabetes mellitus is a form of diabetes that can progress to a more severe subtype characterized as secondary diabetes mellitus with diabetic peripheral angiopathy with gangrene, indicating extensive macrovascular disease and tissue loss. This underlying metabolic disorder is responsible for several microvascular complications. Both mild nonproliferative retinopathy due to secondary diabetes mellitus and retinopathy due to secondary diabetes mellitus represent ocular manifestations caused by the disease, reflecting damage to the retinal vasculature. In addition, nephropathy due to secondary diabetes mellitus denotes renal involvement, with progressive kidney dysfunction resulting from the same diabetic pathology. Together, these relationships highlight that secondary diabetes mellitus not only predisposes patients to peripheral angiopathy and gangrene but also commonly leads to retinal and renal complications, underscoring the need for comprehensive monitoring and management of both macrovascular and microvascular sequelae.

title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2656
content: Mild nonproliferative retinopathy due to secondary diabetes mellitus and nephropathy due to secondary diabetes mellitus are both recognized complications that arise as a direct result of secondary diabetes mellitus. Secondary diabetes mellitus itself is classified as a form of diabetes that is accompanied by diabetic peripheral angiopathy with gangrene, indicating the presence of severe peripheral vascular disease alongside the metabolic disorder. Consequently, patients with secondary diabetes mellitus are at risk for microvascular damage manifesting as mild nonproliferative retinopathy and renal impairment (nephropathy), as well as macrovascular pathology leading to peripheral angiopathy and potential gangrene. Comprehensive management of secondary diabetes mellitus therefore requires vigilant monitoring of ocular and renal function, alongside aggressive treatment of peripheral vascular disease to prevent progression to gangrene and to mitigate overall morbidity.

The presence of these ocular and renal complications highlights the systemic impact of secondary diabetes mellitus, while the associated diabetic peripheral angiopathy with gangrene underscores the need for comprehensive management that addresses both microvascular damage and advanced peripheral vascular disease.
